title: 10 best herbal essential oils for sour taste in mouth
intro_desc: Herbal essential oils for sour taste in mouth are a blend of plant extracts that help alleviate the unpleasant sensation of a sour or bitter taste in the mouth. These oils are often used to treat conditions such as halitosis, bad breath, and oral infections. The best herbal essential oils for sour taste in mouth include Zingiber officinale, Mentha x piperita, Cinnamomum verum, Eucalyptus globulus, Syzygium aromaticum, Cymbopogon citratus, Cinnamomum zeylanicum, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Piper nigrum, and Curcuma longa. If left untreated, this condition can negatively impact one's self-confidence, social interactions, and overall quality of life.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint essential oils helps with sour taste in mouth because of its natural antibacterial properties that quickly neutralize volatile sulfur compounds, which are responsible for causing bad breath and a sour taste. The menthol content in peppermint oil also acts as an expectorant, helping to break down mucus and debris that can contribute to the formation of plaque and tartar on teeth, further reducing the likelihood of sour tastes. Regular use of peppermint essential oils can leave your mouth feeling fresh and clean, eliminating unpleasant odors and leaving a refreshing sensation.
remedy_properties: Digestive aid: Peppermint essential oil helps to relax the muscles in the digestive tract, which can ease symptoms of indigestion and bloating that may cause a sour taste in the mouth. This is because peppermint oil's anti-inflammatory properties reduce inflammation in the gut and alleviate digestive discomfort.

Anti-inflammatory: Peppermint essential oil has potent anti-inflammatory compounds that help to reduce swelling and irritation in the mouth, throat, and tongue, which can contribute to a sour taste. By reducing inflammation, peppermint oil helps to soothe and calm the mucous membranes.

Antimicrobial: Peppermint essential oil's antimicrobial properties can help to combat bacterial overgrowth in the mouth, which can cause bad breath, tooth decay, and other oral health issues that may contribute to a sour taste. By killing bacteria and fungi, peppermint oil promotes a healthy balance of microorganisms in the mouth.
remedy_parts: Roots: Known for their calming and soothing properties, peppermint roots are used to create essential oils that help alleviate sourness and discomfort in the mouth.
Barks: The barks of peppermint plants contain compounds that have anti-inflammatory effects, making them useful for reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with sour taste.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh peppermint leaves at dawn when the oil content is highest and dry them thoroughly for 3 days.
Crush dried peppermint leaves into fine powder using mortar and pestle for 10 minutes.
Steam distill crushed peppermint powder in a still for 45-60 minutes to extract essential oil.
Collect and filter the resulting essential oil through coffee filter paper and store it in dark glass bottle for up to 6 months.
Mix 5-7 drops of freshly prepared peppermint essential oil with water or mouthwash according to your taste and use as oral rinse twice daily.

Barks: Contain natural antibacterial agents that aid in reducing bacteria growth and relieving sour taste sensations.
remedy_recipe: Harvest mature eucalyptus globulus leaves fresh from the tree.
Clean and dry the harvested leaves to prevent contamination and spoilage.
Load the dried leaves into a distillation apparatus designed for essential oil production.
Steam the eucalyptus leaves at high pressure to release the aromatic compounds, monitoring temperature and flow rates.
Collect the resulting essential oil by condensing the steam vapor and separating it from the water fraction.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 cups of fresh salvia officinalis leaves from a healthy plant grown without pesticides or fertilizers.
Dry the harvested leaves by spreading them thinly on paper towels or a wire rack for 7-10 days.
Use a steam distillation apparatus to extract the essential oil from 100g of dried salvia officinalis leaves with 500ml of water at atmospheric pressure.
Collect and filter the resulting essential oil using a coffee filter or cheesecloth into a clean glass bottle, discarding any sediment or impurities.
Store the finished salvia officinalis essential oil in an amber glass bottle tightly sealed in a cool dark place for up to 6 months before use.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried thymus vulgaris leaves in the early morning for optimal oil quality.
Chop 20g of freshly harvested thymus vulgaris leaves into small pieces to ensure efficient extraction.

Store the mixture in a cool, dark place for 14 days to allow for slow and gentle extraction of essential oils.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh origanum majorana leaves measuring 20-25 grams.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh cymbopogon citratus leaves and rinse them thoroughly under cold running water.

Distill the mixture in a rotary evaporator at 40°c for 2 hours, collecting the essential oil that separates from the hydrosol.
Filter the essential oil through a coffee filter paper into a dark glass bottle and store it in a cool dry place.

By promoting a healthy oral environment, Cinnamomum zeylanicum essential oils may provide relief from sour tastes in the mouth.
remedy_constituents: Cinnamaldehyde: It helps reduce the sensation of sour taste by blocking the sensory receptors in the mouth that detect sourness, thereby providing relief from sour taste.

Cinnamic acid: It has anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, which help to reduce inflammation and combat the underlying causes of sour taste, such as bacterial or fungal infections in the mouth.
Eugenol: It has analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce pain and inflammation in the mouth, thereby providing relief from sour taste and other discomforts associated with it.
remedy_parts: Barks: Cinnamon bark essential oil is commonly used to help alleviate sour taste due to its antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties.
Leaves: Cinnamon leaf essential oil is used to help counteract sour taste by its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, which help soothe and calm the mouth.

Rhyzomes: Cinnamon rhizome essential oil, also known as Ceylon cinnamon oil, is used to help neutralize sour taste due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that help reduce inflammation and infection.
remedy_recipe: Harvest cinnamomum zeylanicum leaves and twigs from mature trees at the peak of aromatic oil production.
Steam distill the harvested plant material at 100-110 degrees celsius for 3-4 hours.
Collect the resulting essential oil from the distillation process and transfer it to a clean container.
Filter the essential oil through a paper filter or cotton wool to remove any impurities immediately.
Store the cinnamomum zeylanicum essential oil in a dark glass bottle with a tight-fitting lid safely.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra essential oils helps with sour taste in mouth because of its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. The oil contains glycyrrhizin, a compound that has been shown to reduce inflammation in the oral cavity and prevent the growth of bacteria that can cause bad breath and a sour taste. Additionally, its soothing properties can help calm the mucous membranes in the mouth, reducing irritation and discomfort associated with a sour taste. This makes it a natural remedy for maintaining good oral health.
remedy_constituents: Saponins: Help neutralize acidity and reduce inflammation in the mouth, thereby alleviating sour taste.
Flavonoids: Act as antioxidants and anti-inflammatory agents, which help soothe and calm the mucous membranes in the mouth, reducing irritation and sour taste.

Terpenoids (specifically, Ursolic acid): Exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help reduce inflammation and soothe the mucous membranes in the mouth, thereby alleviating sour taste.
remedy_parts: Roots: They are commonly used to make essential oils due to their high content of glycyrrhizin, which has anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties that can help reduce oral inflammation and prevent sour taste.
Leaves: The leaves of Glycyrrhiza glabra contain flavonoids and other compounds that have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe oral inflammation and address sour taste.

Flowers: Glycyrrhiza glabra flowers contain flavonoids, terpenoids, and other compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce oral inflammation and prevent sour taste.
remedy_recipe: Steam distill 100 kg of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots in 200 liters of distilled water for 3 hours.
Collect the steam distilled essential oil and separate it from the water using a separator.
Filter the collected essential oil through a 0.2 micron filter paper to remove impurities.
Store the filtered essential oil in a dark glass bottle with a tight-fitting lid in a cool place.
Label and date the bottle and store it in a well-ventilated area away from direct sunlight.

remedies.remedy_desc: Piper nigrum essential oils helps with sour taste in mouth because of its natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. The oil's active compound, piperine, has been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria and yeast that can cause bad breath and sour taste. Additionally, piperine's ability to reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat helps to alleviate symptoms of sour taste and promotes a healthy oral environment. This makes piper nigrum essential oil a popular natural remedy for maintaining good oral health.
remedy_constituents: Eugenol: Eugenol has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the mouth, which may be contributing to a sour taste.
Borneol: Borneol has antimicrobial properties that can help combat the growth of bacteria that can cause bad breath and a sour taste in the mouth.
Limonene: Limonene has antioxidant properties that can help neutralize free radicals and reduce the production of acids in the mouth, which can contribute to a sour taste.
remedy_parts: Seeds: They are used to make essential oils due to their high concentration of piperine, which is responsible for the pungent and sour taste.
Fruits (specifically, unripe fruits): They contain a higher concentration of piperine than other parts of the plant, which contributes to the sour taste in essential oils.
Buds: They are used to make essential oils as they have a higher concentration of piperine than the mature fruits, which is responsible for the pungent and sour taste.
remedy_recipe: Harvest ripe piper nigrum berries from a well-maintained and disease-free plantation in late summer.
Steam distill the freshly harvested berries in a ratio of 1:2 with distilled water for 2 hours.
Collect the condensed vapor containing the essential oil and separate it from the distillation water.
Filter the obtained essential oil through a paper filter to remove any impurities or solid particles.
Store the filtered essential oil in dark glass bottles sealed with a tight-fitting cork or stopper.

remedies.remedy_desc: Curcuma longa essential oils helps with sour taste in mouth because of its potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. It contains curcumin, a bioactive compound that inhibits the growth of bacteria and fungi responsible for causing bad breath and a sour taste. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory properties help reduce swelling and irritation in the mouth, which can contribute to a sour taste. Regular use of Curcuma longa essential oils may help freshen breath and reduce the sensation of a sour taste in the mouth.
remedy_constituents: Curcuminoids: They have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce inflammation in the mouth and neutralize acidic compounds that cause a sour taste.
Furanocoumarins: These compounds have antimicrobial properties that help prevent the growth of bacteria in the mouth that can cause an unpleasant sour taste.
Volatiles (Caryophyllene, Limonene): These terpene compounds have antibacterial and antifungal properties that help control the growth of microorganisms in the mouth that can cause a sour taste.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes are the most used part of Curcuma longa for essential oils due to their high concentration of turmeric oil, which has anti-inflammatory properties.
Roots: Roots are used for essential oils as they contain a high amount of turmerone, which helps in reducing inflammation and has a sour taste in the mouth.
Leaves: Leaves are another part used for essential oils as they contain eugenol, which has antibacterial properties and helps in reducing the sour taste in the mouth.
remedy_recipe: Harvest mature curcuma longa rhizomes within 2-3 months after flowering to ensure maximum oil content.
Dry the rhizomes in a well-ventilated area for 7-10 days to reduce moisture content to 10-12 percent.
Steam distill the dried rhizomes at 100-110°c for 3-4 hours to extract the essential oil.
Collect and separate the essential oil from the distilled water using a separator or decanter.
Filter the essential oil through a paper or cloth filter to remove impurities and improve clarity.
